Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered, even though I tried to make it work. I'd usually set aside time in the evenings when my roommates were quieter, and I'd listen to lecture recordings while doing other things. With 14 online courses, I had to juggle a lot, so I'd prioritize based on what was due soonest. I aimed for about 16 hours a week total, but some weeks it felt like I was just putting out fires rather than actually learning deeply.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I tried to have some consistency, like Sunday afternoons for catching up on videos, but it rarely stuck. When assignments piled up or my extracurriculars demanded more time, studying became reactive rather than planned. I'd go days without touching certain courses, then cram everything in before a deadline hit.
Q3: Honestly, deadlines were my main trigger for logging in. I'd get an email notification about something due, and that would push me to open the LMS. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, especially since my attendance in live sessions was really low. The fear of falling behind was usually what got me there, not genuine curiosity or excitement.
Q4: There was a brief period mid-semester where checking the LMS became more automatic for me. I started logging in every morning with my coffee, just scrolling through announcements. It happened because I'd missed a deadline and felt terrible about it, so I made it a habit to at least check in daily. But that routine faded when things got overwhelming again and I had to prioritize other responsibilities.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I could replay sections I didn't understand, pause to take notes, and listen while doing other tasks. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives and questions made me feel less alone in my confusion. Those two resources together probably carried me through the course.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials and supplementary PDFs, to be honest. I always told myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. They felt overwhelming compared to just listening to a lecture, and with so many courses, I had to cut corners somewhere. The interactive quizzes that weren't graded also got ignored because they felt like extra work without immediate payoff.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to something I'd be graded on or if it genuinely helped me understand a confusing concept. When I could see the immediate relevance, I'd engage more deeply. Peer discussions were worth it because they made me feel connected and supported, which I needed emotionally. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to my success, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With required work already piling up, anything labeled optional went straight to the bottom of my list. There was guilt about skipping them because I knew they might help, but I had to be realistic about my energy and time. Required activities carried weight and consequences, so they got my attention first, even if I was exhausted.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my commute or while exercising. I also had a study group with some peers from the course where we'd meet in person and work through problems together. My notes were handwritten in a physical notebook, which helped me process information better than typing ever did.
Q10: My peer study sessions were huge for my learning but completely invisible to the LMS. We'd meet twice a week at the library, working through concepts and quizzing each other verbally. I also spent a lot of time just thinking through problems while walking or doing chores, processing what I'd heard in lectures. The LMS would show I watched a video once, but not the three days I spent mentally wrestling with that content.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access a lecture or reading once to download it, then study it extensively offline over the following days. My watch time probably looked low because I'd download videos and play them through a different app. The LMS only captured that initial access, not the hours I spent with the material afterward.
Q12: There was a stretch around midterms where I felt completely overwhelmed and sad. I was dealing with some personal stuff back home, and my motivation just tanked. Even though my stress wasn't unbearable, this persistent sadness made everything feel heavier than it should. My attendance dropped even lower during that time, and I relied heavily on my peer support network to keep going.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or had competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars, the LMS became something I'd rush through rather than engage with meaningfully. I'd skim discussion posts instead of reading them carefully, or I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to mark them complete. The workload across all 14 courses meant I was constantly triaging, and sometimes this course lost that battle.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about other assignments or personal issues. I'd scroll through forum posts without really absorbing what people were saying. Physically I was present in the LMS, but mentally I was checked out. Those sessions felt like going through motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I postponed working on assignments even when I knew they were important, especially the ones that felt overwhelming or unclear. The sadness I was carrying made it hard to start things that required mental energy. I'd tell myself I'd feel more motivated tomorrow, but tomorrow would come with its own challenges. It wasn't laziness—it was more like emotional paralysis that I had to push through.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my LMS usage. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, logging in multiple times, watching videos I'd been putting off, posting in discussions to meet participation requirements. Without deadlines, I might have drifted away entirely. They were stressful but also necessary scaffolding that kept me somewhat on track.
Q17: When I got feedback on assignments, it definitely affected my engagement afterward. Positive feedback gave me a boost and made me want to try harder on the next thing. Negative or confusing feedback sometimes made me want to disengage because it felt discouraging. The timing mattered too—feedback that came quickly was more useful than feedback weeks later when I'd already moved on mentally.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which was both good and bad. Good because I could work around my schedule and other commitments. Bad because without rigid structure, I could let things slide. The weekly modules helped me know what to focus on, but when modules overlapped or piled up, I'd fall behind. More frequent smaller deadlines might have kept me more consistent.
Q19: The discussion forums were designed in a way that made engagement easier—they felt conversational and low-stakes. But the sheer volume of content across modules made engagement harder because I never felt like I could truly catch up. Also, having to navigate between so many different sections of the LMS was confusing sometimes. A clearer, simpler layout would have helped me stay focused.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious and keeps checking the platform without actually being able to focus on work. They might be opening materials, reading instructions repeatedly, but feeling too overwhelmed to produce anything. Or they could be searching for clarity they never find, stuck in a loop of activity without progress. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually describes me somewhat. My LMS activity was moderate, but I did a lot of learning offline through downloaded materials and peer study sessions. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't engaged, but I was—just not in ways the system could track. Performance can come from deep engagement with materials accessed briefly online but studied extensively elsewhere.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like when someone logs in, how long they spend on pages, and whether they complete tasks. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether someone was actually thinking deeply or just going through motions. They also miss offline studying, emotional state, peer collaboration, and all the mental processing that happens away from the screen.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to check boxes. It means thinking about concepts outside of study time, connecting ideas across different parts of my life, and feeling curious rather than just obligated. For me, engagement also includes feeling connected to peers and supported in the learning process. It's mental, emotional, and social all together.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much my peer support network carried me through difficult weeks, or how listening to lectures while walking helped me process information better than sitting at a desk. It wouldn't capture the sadness I was working through or how hard I had to fight some days just to open my laptop. My resilience and the emotional labor of staying in the course—none of that shows up in click data.
